era_context: The Warring States period in China is in its final phase, with Qin Shihuang's forces rapidly consolidating control over rival states. In the Mediterranean, the Hellenistic world is dominated by the Seleucid and Ptolemaic empires, with Rome consolidating its control of Italy. The Second Punic War is still several years away (218 BCE).

events.title: Qin forces conquer Han state
events.description: Qin forces conquer the Han state, one of the remaining independent Warring States. Han, located in central China, had been a significant power during the Warring States period. Its conquest brings vast territories under Qin control and represents another major milestone in Qin Shihuang's unification campaign. The conquest of Han is followed by the conquest of Zhao, Chu, and other remaining states, leading to the completion of unification in 221 BCE.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Qin administrative records and archaeological evidence

events.certainty_note: The conquest of Han is attested in the Shiji and corroborated by archaeological evidence. However, the precise dating of this conquest within the 225–221 BCE period is uncertain. The Shiji's chronology is generally reliable but may have dating uncertainties of ±2–3 years for individual campaigns.

events.title: Seleucus II Callinicus continues reign; internal conflicts persist
events.description: Seleucus II Callinicus continues his reign over the Seleucid Empire during a period of internal strife and territorial loss. His reign (246–222 BCE) is marked by conflict with his brother Antiochus Hierax and the loss of control over Bactria and other eastern provinces. By 224 BCE, the Seleucid Empire is significantly weakened by these internal conflicts. Seleucus II will die in 222 BCE, and his brother Antiochus III will attempt to restore Seleucid power.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus II's reign dates are well-established in Polybius and later sources. The year 224 BCE falls within his reign, which ended in 222 BCE.

events.title: Ptolemy III Euergetes reigns over Ptolemaic Egypt
events.description: Ptolemy III Euergetes continues his reign over Ptolemaic Egypt, a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing. His reign (246–221 BCE) is marked by military campaigns in Syria and Anatolia, though by 224 BCE he is in the final years of his reign. Ptolemy III is known for his patronage of the Library of Alexandria and his military campaigns against the Seleucid Empire. His death in 221 BCE will be followed by the accession of his son Ptolemy IV Philopator.
key_figures: Arsinoe II (Queen, wife of Ptolemy II; still venerated)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy III's reign dates are well-established through papyrological evidence and Greek historical sources. The year 224 BCE falls within his reign, which ended in 221 BCE.

events.title: Rome consolidates control of Italy; tensions with Carthage escalate
events.description: Rome continues to consolidate its control over the Italian peninsula and expand its influence in the western Mediterranean. Tensions with Carthage are escalating, particularly over control of Spain and Sicily. By 224 BCE, Rome is the dominant power in Italy and is beginning to challenge Carthaginian hegemony in the western Mediterranean. These tensions will lead to the outbreak of the Second Punic War in 218 BCE.
events.certainty_note: Roman consolidation of Italy and escalating tensions with Carthage are well-documented in classical sources, but the specific events of 224 BCE are less precisely dated. The Second Punic War officially began in 218 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the conquest of Han is uncertain; some sources place it in 225 BCE or 223 BCE rather than 224 BCE. The Shiji's chronology for the Warring States period is generally reliable but may have minor dating uncertainties of ±2–3 years for individual campaigns. The precise sequence of events in the pre-war period before the Second Punic War is sometimes unclear in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Chinese sources are dominated by the Shiji, compiled centuries after the events. Archaeological evidence from Qin-period administrative documents provides corroboration but is fragmentary. For the Mediterranean, Polybius and Livy are primary sources, both written well after the events. Egyptian sources are primarily administrative papyri. The Seleucid Empire is documented primarily through Greek and later sources. All ancient sources on Rome and Carthage are written by Romans or later authors dependent on Roman sources, introducing potential bias.

graph_edges.note: Conquest of Han is followed by conquest of Zhao in the unification campaign
Seleucus II's reign ends with his death and Antiochus III's accession
Ptolemy III's reign ends with his death and Ptolemy IV's accession
Roman consolidation and escalating tensions with Carthage lead to the Second Punic War
